meta.title: Nagila Decastro
meta.description: Mother of two girls, wife, homeschool educator, and mentor to mothers. Sleep consulting, real motherhood, homeschool, and METI Mentorship.

quote.text: The home cared for first holds everything else up.

body: I am Nagila — mother to Helena and Catharina, wife to Ewerton, living on Cape Cod. Every birth rewrote what I understood about rest, presence and home.
I learned firsthand that a baby's sleep is not a technical detail — it is the oxygen of an entire family. When a mother sleeps again, she returns to herself.

I have homeschooled my two daughters from day one. Not as a rejection of the world, but as a deep yes to time — the time that does not come twice, the time it takes to pass character on at the child's own pace.

Today I walk alongside mothers and families who want more than to survive motherhood and parenting — backed by serious training, not guesswork. Families who want to live inside it all with clarity, ease and purpose.
Helena arrived in 2021. Catharina in 2024. Ewerton in 2017.
Before any mother I walk alongside, there is this home. It is the home I speak from, the one I return to, the place where everything I teach was first lived.

services.sectionSubtitle: Four mentorship fronts, one root: caring for the mother and the home is caring for everything that comes after.

cards.description: A human, personalized plan for your baby's sleep — honoring the bond, the child's temperament and your family's real rhythm. No harsh formulas, no guilt.
cards.whatsappText: Hi Nagila, I came from your site — I'd like to know more about Sleep Consulting

detail.intro: If you're here, sleep is not happening. Maybe it never has. Maybe it did for a while and then regressed. Maybe you can't even remember the last full night you slept — and you're exhausted enough that you don't know what to try anymore.
whyBody: A baby's sleep is not a technical detail. It is the oxygen of the family. Sleep-deprived mothers lose clarity, patience and self-trust — and the relationship with the partner, the other children, and their own faith silently pays the price.
It is not weakness, it is physiology. And there is a real path forward — grounded in infant sleep neuroscience and respect for the bond, with no rigid method and no promise of the impossible.
journeyBody: I listen first. I don't arrive with a ready-made plan for your baby — I arrive with questions to understand the bond, your child's temperament, and the realistic rhythm of your home. Individual assessment before any technique: every baby is a baby.
I work as a Specialized Sleep Consultant with serious training — no letting the baby cry it out, no one-size-fits-all protocol. Sleep and behavior are not separate problems; I treat them together.
Then we leave with an honest plan: what you do tonight, what we adjust next week, what we revisit together. Ongoing support throughout the process, not a one-time consult.
forWhomItems: Mothers of babies and young children from 0 to 5 years
Mothers tired of method X, method Y, formulas that ignore who your child actually is
Mothers who do not want to use cry-it-out and are looking for a respectful path
Mothers who want to sleep again without giving up who they are
detail.ctaWhatsappText: Hi Nagila, I came from the Sleep Consulting page — I'd like to talk about my baby's sleep

cards.description: Mentorship for mothers in transition: first child, second child, identity after birth, marriage after baby. Honest talk, practical tools, gentle support.
cards.whatsappText: Hi Nagila, I came from your site — I'd like to know more about Real Motherhood Mentorship

detail.intro: Motherhood is not a miracle that happens to you. It is a crossing you cross — and almost no one is honest about what it's like from the inside. Identity that comes apart. Marriage that shifts. Body that returns different. Career on pause. Friends who disappear. And the silent question: "am I still me?".
whyBody: When motherhood is lived alone, it becomes survival. And mothers who survive for too long eventually collapse — into exhaustion, into anger, into distance.

It is not moral weakness. It is the absence of company, and sometimes the absence of information — many postpartum experiences feel like personal chaos when they are actually well-studied physiology and developmental psychology. You just need someone who recognizes it, guides you, and stays near.
A mother needs another mother who has been through it, who has cried in the shower, who has come back to herself — with foundation, not only with affection.
journeyBody: We talk. Really talk. This isn't spreadsheet coaching, it's woman-to-woman mentorship — grounded in my own experience as mother to Helena and Catharina, plus my training in parental education and developmental psychology under Thuyanna Mallu.

We work together on what is weighing right now: identity in postpartum, marriage after the baby, accumulated exhaustion, mother-guilt without reason and with reason, decisions between work and motherhood, returning to faith and to the friendships motherhood shook, building a routine for when there is more than one child in the house.
Regular meetings, no tight deadline, no rigid method. I listen, I ask questions back, I give you concrete tools — and I come back the next week, and the week after, until you no longer need me.
forWhomItems: First-time mothers feeling lost in the middle of the obvious
Second-child mothers who feel they are starting from zero, and guilty about it
Mothers who lost themselves in the work of raising people
Mothers who feel the marriage has been pushed to second place and don't know how to come back
Mothers who want to be whole — not only mothers
detail.ctaWhatsappText: Hi Nagila, I came from the Real Motherhood Mentorship page — I'd like to talk

cards.description: For mothers who feel the call to homeschool but don't know where to start. Routine, materials, a simple curriculum and the courage for the first week — and all the weeks after.
cards.whatsappText: Hi Nagila, I came from your site — I'd like to know more about Homeschool Mentorship

detail.intro: You feel the call to educate at home. You've felt it since before the first child, or you started feeling it now that they're school age. But you don't know where to start — and every time you ask, someone scares you with paperwork, curriculum, isolation, or lack of time.
whyBody: Homeschooling is not a rejection of the world. It is a deep yes to time — the time to learn at the child's own pace, the time to build a bond while teaching, the time to pass on values that don't fit into a school schedule.
I have homeschooled my two daughters from day one, and it is the decision that has most redesigned our family. It is not easy. It is worth every hour.

And yes, it can be done with ease — without becoming a full-time private tutor, without losing the joy of being a mother, without isolating the family from the world. But it requires method, not improvisation.
journeyBody: I don't hand you a ready-made curriculum, because every home is different. I help you design yours — starting with the WHY (the values you want to pass on) before the HOW (subjects, method, materials).

We cover together: legal and paperwork basics where you live; choosing the teaching approach (classical, Charlotte Mason, eclectic, unschool — whichever fits your family); building the real daily and weekly routine; free and paid resources to start light; how to integrate different ages when you have more than one child; the father's role in homeschool; what to do with a small baby while the older child is studying; strategies for the weeks when you think you'll quit.
Continuous mentorship, not a one-off class — so you have someone in the first week, the fifth, and the twentieth, when the excitement fades and the method has to carry the home.
forWhomItems: Mothers considering homeschool for the first time
Families who have started but feel lost
Mothers already homeschooling who feel alone in the journey
Families with more than one child trying to balance different ages
Couples where one parent is still hesitant and needs serious information
Christian families wanting to align faith and education inside their own home
detail.ctaWhatsappText: Hi Nagila, I came from the Homeschool Mentorship page — I'd like to talk about educating at home

cards.description: Method of Intentional Traditional Education. Parental education that recovers clear limits, family hierarchy and character formation — with the intentionality modern parenting forgot.
cards.whatsappText: Hi Nagila, I came from your site — I'd like to know more about METI Mentorship (Parental Education)

detail.intro: METI — Method of Intentional Traditional Education — is the methodology developed by Thuyanna Mallu as a serious answer to the reactive parenting of our time. I am a Certified Parental Educator in this method, and I bring it into your family: raising as before, but choosing every gesture, grounded in neuroscience, in virtue and in order.
whyBody: Most families today raise by improvisation. They react to children's behavior, copy what they saw at home, outsource to school or screen. The result is a generation of exhausted parents and anxious children without direction.

METI rests on pillars modern parenting has unlearned: parental authority in place of endless negotiation; order and predictability that give the child emotional security; character formation through the cardinal virtues — courage, prudence, justice, temperance — rather than reward for good behavior; limits set with firmness and respect, never with emotional punishment.
Tradition is not blind repetition. It is tested wisdom we choose to honor with intention, gesture by gesture.
journeyBody: We start from your real family — the temperament of each child, the rhythm of your marriage, what is already working and what is draining you. Not theory from a manual: every home enters differently.

We apply the pillars of METI to your daily life: how to return authority without becoming authoritarian, how to build daily liturgies (meals, prayer, sleep, rest) that carry the home on their own, how to raise for character without outsourcing to school, screen, or luck.
Regular meetings, continuous adjustments, real-life accompaniment — not loose theory in a workbook.
forWhomItems: Parents who feel raising has become 24-hour negotiation
Families who have seen modern parenting form anxious children without direction
Couples who want to align how they raise before the children grow up picking sides
Families of faith who want education to reflect what they believe, lived at home every day
Parents who want to return to the intentionality they saw in their grandparents, adapted to today
